channel.title: The Corridor — African Tourism & Geopolitics Intelligence
channel.description: Weekly geopolitical tourism intelligence for Africa. Every Monday, sharp analysis at the intersection of global politics and African tourism strategy — for tourism ministers, national tourism boards, investors and industry leaders.

item.title: When China Opens the Door, East Africa Must Decide What to Sell
item.description: China has announced zero tariffs for 53 African countries effective May 2026. Chinese Vice President Han Zheng visited Nairobi this week. Kenya is targeting one million Chinese tourists against 105,000 arrivals in 2025. The Corridor analyses whether East Africa has the Sovereign Tourism Architecture to convert the diplomatic moment into sustained high-yield source market revenue.

item.title: When Casablanca Builds the Continent's New Gateway
item.description: Royal Air Maroc is adding 10 new international routes in 2026, including the first direct Africa to US Pacific Coast flight in history. A $1.6 billion terminal is under construction. Morocco received 19.8 million visitors in 2025. The Mobility Corridor is shifting decisively northward.

item.title: When China Opens the Door, East Africa Must Decide What to Sell
item.description: China has announced zero tariffs for 53 African countries effective May 2026. Chinese Vice President Han Zheng visited Nairobi this week. Kenya is targeting one million Chinese tourists against 105,000 arrivals in 2025. The Corridor analyses East Africa's sovereign tourism diplomacy architecture gap and three interventions that cannot wait for the next FOCAC summit.

item.title: When Casablanca Builds the Continent's New Gateway
item.description: Royal Air Maroc is adding 10 new international routes in 2026 including the first direct Africa to US Pacific Coast flight. A $1.6 billion terminal is under construction. Morocco received 19.8 million visitors in 2025. The Mobility Corridor connecting Europe and the Americas to Africa is shifting decisively northward.

item.title: When the Migration Loses Its Calendar: East Africa's Most Valuable Tourism Product Is Becoming Unbookable
item.dc:creator: The Corridor
item.description: The Great Migration is losing its calendar. 90 percent of the Mara-Loita wildebeest migration footprint has been lost since 2020. ICPAC has issued a live flood warning covering the exact circuits that drive East Africa's peak season revenue. The Corridor analyses the sovereign economic risk and the three strategic moves East Africa cannot defer.
item.content:encoded:
The Great Migration generates hundreds of millions of dollars annually for Kenya and Tanzania. Yet 90 percent of the Mara-Loita migration footprint has been lost since 2020 due to fencing and land subdivision. A live ICPAC forecast projects a 45 percent probability of wetter-than-normal conditions across East Africa between March and May 2026. A temperature rise of 4.8 to 5.8 degrees Celsius over six decades is making the Migration structurally unreliable as a bookable product. East Africa does not have a wildlife problem. It has a corridor governance problem.

Read the full analysis →

item.title: When a Neighbour Builds a Continent's Gateway: Ethiopia's Bishoftu Gambit
item.dc:creator: The Corridor
item.description: Ethiopia is constructing the Bishoftu International Airport at a projected cost of $5 billion, with a design capacity of 100 million passengers per year. This is not a domestic infrastructure project. It is a continental positioning strategy. The Corridor introduces four strategic moves Nairobi cannot defer.
item.content:encoded:
Ethiopia is constructing the Bishoftu International Airport at a projected cost of $5 billion, with a design capacity of 100 million passengers per year. This is not a domestic infrastructure project. It is a continental positioning strategy. When Bishoftu opens, Ethiopian Airlines will control the primary hub for intra-African and Africa-to-world aviation at a scale no other African carrier can match for at least a decade.

Read the full analysis →

item.title: When the Debt Comes Due, Tourism Pays the Price
item.dc:creator: The Corridor
item.description: Kenya owes $9.1 billion in external debt repayments in 2026. Rwanda is restructuring IMF obligations. Tanzania faces a 22 percent currency depreciation that has erased the dollar-denominated margins of its lodge sector. These are not isolated fiscal events. They are symptoms of a structural condition across East Africa.
item.content:encoded:
Kenya owes $9.1 billion in external debt repayments in 2026. Rwanda is restructuring IMF obligations. Tanzania faces a 22 percent currency depreciation that has erased the dollar-denominated margins of its lodge sector. These are not isolated fiscal events. They are symptoms of a structural condition across East Africa in which tourism revenue is being deployed to service sovereign debt rather than build the infrastructure that generates future visitor demand.

Read the full analysis →

item.title: When the Gulf Goes Dark, Africa Must Turn Its Lights On
item.dc:creator: The Corridor
item.description: The 2026 US-Israel-Iran conflict has erased 23 to 38 million tourists from global travel forecasts and up to $56 billion in visitor spend. These tourists have not disappeared. They have been displaced. Africa is the only continent structurally, experientially and narratively positioned to absorb this demand.
item.content:encoded:
The 2026 US-Israel-Iran conflict has erased 23 to 38 million tourists from global travel forecasts and up to $56 billion in visitor spend. The airports of Dubai, Doha and Abu Dhabi, collectively responsible for 14 percent of all global international transit activity, are operating at severely restricted capacity. These tourists have not disappeared. They have been displaced. Africa is the only continent structurally, experientially and narratively positioned to absorb this demand.

Read the full analysis →

item.title: The $2 Billion Structural Leak: Why East Africa's Tourism Economy Fails Its Own People
item.dc:creator: The Corridor
item.description: Africa attracted 74 million international tourists in 2024, generating $169 billion in total economic contribution. Yet a structural analysis of where that revenue actually flows reveals a consistent pattern of value extraction that undermines the sovereign tourism architecture of the continent's most visited destinations.
item.content:encoded:
Africa attracted 74 million international tourists in 2024, generating $169 billion in total economic contribution. Yet a structural analysis of where that revenue actually flows reveals a consistent pattern of value extraction that undermines the sovereign tourism architecture of the continent's most visited destinations. The Corridor introduces the Corridor Index, a framework for measuring the degree to which a destination captures, retains and reinvests visitor spend within its own economy.

Read the full analysis →
